summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her experience recording the audiobook version of 'Weapons of Math Destruction' at Random House studios. She describes the challenges of voice recording, including retakes for flat tone and difficult phrases. Despite initial worries about finding typos or outdated content, she expresses satisfaction with the book while bracing for future negative reviews. She also announces the book's new website featuring early blurbs and a positive Publisher's Weekly review.
key_points: Recording an audiobook is surprisingly difficult, requiring many retakes for tone and emphasis
Certain phrases (like 'assist statistics') are nearly impossible to say aloud
The author is pleased with the book's content but anxiously anticipates negative reviews
The book has received positive early coverage including a Publisher's Weekly review
money_quotes: I'm constantly having to retake sentences, either because I thought my tone was too flat (I hate flat audiobook readers!), or wasn't emphasizing the right words, or because the words are just hard to say.
I promise to never, ever write the phrase 'assist statistics' in anything that might someday be read out loud, ever, anywhere.
I'm happy with the book! Is that ok to say (not out loud!!)? I'm holding on to this delicious feeling until the nasty reviews come out.
After that I'll just cry inside at all times.
key_insight: Even authors who write confidently about weighty topics like algorithmic bias experience vulnerability and anxiety when their work faces public judgment.
